title: [lichen_util.h:50] secure_zero NULL check returns silently instead of asserting
description:
In secure_zero(), the function silently returns when given NULL or zero length:

```c
static inline void secure_zero(void *ptr, size_t len)
{
    if (ptr == NULL || len == 0) return;
    ...
}
```

For a security-critical function, silently accepting invalid input could mask bugs in calling code. If a caller passes NULL expecting memory to be zeroed, the bug goes undetected.

Options:
1. Current: Silent return (caller error masked)
2. Assert: __ASSERT(ptr != NULL, ...) - crashes in debug, silent in release
3. Log warning: Still safe but alerts to bug

len == 0 is debatable - it's valid to ask to zero zero bytes. But ptr == NULL is always a bug.

Impact: P2 - could mask security bugs in callers. A caller might think sensitive data was zeroed when it wasn't.
close_reason: Fixed: Added __ASSERT for NULL ptr check in secure_zero; debug builds will now catch bugs while release builds maintain safe silent return
